title: SP-LI-001 · Patients Are Searching
subtitle: Dr Hugo Stephenson · Pharma
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-001
**Author:** Dr Hugo Stephenson, CEO, TrialScreen
**Series:** Patients Are Searching
**Target Segment:** Pharma (VP Clinical Operations / Head of Patient Recruitment)
**Posting Guidance:** Post Monday morning Week 1 — anchor post for entire campaign. Pin to profile for 30 days.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

Patients are searching. Right now.

Not waiting for a doctor referral. Not waiting for a pharmaceutical ad to find them. Searching — on their phones, in hospital waiting rooms, at 2am when the fear gets loudest.

We track search behaviour across trialscreen.org, which lists more than 120,000 actively recruiting studies in over 200 countries. The signal we're seeing is consistent and it's been consistent for years: patients with serious and rare conditions are far more motivated to find trials than the clinical research industry typically assumes.

And yet enrollment remains the single biggest bottleneck in drug development. Roughly 85% of trials fail to meet their enrollment targets on time. Roughly 80% of trials experience delays. The average Phase III trial takes 15 years from investigational compound to approval.

There's a gap here that doesn't add up.

If patients are searching — and the data says they are — why are so many trials struggling to find them?

Part of the answer is discoverability. Most trial listings are written in protocol language that no patient without a medical degree can parse. Search algorithms surface results that match clinical terminology, not the plain-language terms patients actually use. A patient searching "joint pain that won't go away" is unlikely to find a trial listed as "a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study of a novel JAK inhibitor in adults with active rheumatoid arthritis."

Part of the answer is trust. Patients have been burned by healthcare systems. Communities with historically low participation rates — Black, Indigenous, Latino, older adult, rural — have documented reasons to be cautious. Recruitment that feels transactional will fail with these populations every time.

And part of the answer is speed. When a patient does find a trial and reaches out, the average response time from sites is measured in days or weeks. Our own data from FLOW, our pre-screening product, consistently shows that patients who are not contacted within 72 hours of expressing interest are effectively lost. They found another option, or they gave up.

The good news: all three of these problems are solvable. We're not at the mercy of fate here.

What I've been thinking about lately is whether the industry is asking the right question. The question "how do we find more patients?" may be less useful than "how do we make it easier for patients who are already looking to find us?"

That reframe changes everything — the technology you invest in, the language you use on listings, the speed at which you follow up, the trust infrastructure you build before someone is ever eligible.

I'll be writing about each of these over the coming weeks. This is the first post in a series I'm calling Patients Are Searching.

If this resonates — or if you're seeing something different in your trials — I'd genuinely welcome the conversation.

---

title: SP-LI-002 · Trial Discoverability Index
subtitle: Dr Hugo Stephenson · Pharma
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-002
**Author:** Dr Hugo Stephenson, CEO, TrialScreen
**Series:** Trial Discoverability Index
**Target Segment:** Pharma (VP Clinical Operations / Head of Patient Recruitment)
**Posting Guidance:** Post Wednesday Week 1, two days after SP-LI-001. References prior post to build series continuity.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

53.9%.

That's the proportion of patients with rare diseases who first learned about their clinical trial options through an online search — not through their physician, not through a patient advocacy group, not through a hospital referral system.

We're going to publish the full data in our inaugural Trial Discoverability Index next week. But I wanted to lead with this number because it challenges a core assumption embedded in how clinical trial recruitment is typically funded and designed.

The dominant model of trial recruitment was built for a world where the physician is the gatekeeper. Sponsor outreach goes to investigators. Investigators refer patients. The patient is a passive recipient of information. In that model, spending on patient-facing digital infrastructure feels optional — a nice-to-have, not a strategic necessity.

53.9% says that model is broken. Or at least: it's incomplete.

For rare diseases in particular, the patient is often the expert in the room. Someone living with scleroderma, or a paediatric cancer, or an ultra-rare metabolic condition has typically done more research about their disease than any generalist physician they've seen. They found their support community online. They found their specialist online. And yes — they're finding their clinical trials online.

What follows from this?

First: your trial listing is a patient touchpoint. The way it's written, the search terms it's indexed against, the trust signals it carries — these matter. Protocol-language listings written for IRB submission are not the same as listings written to be found by a patient at 11pm.

Second: the speed of follow-up after a patient expresses interest matters enormously. Digital search creates intent. If that intent isn't met with a warm, timely response, it evaporates. We're seeing 60%+ drop-off in patient interest when first contact doesn't happen within 72 hours.

Third: the patient search journey is measurable. We can tell you — for a given condition, in a given geography — what patients are searching for, what language they use, and how many potential participants are actively looking right now.

Full Trial Discoverability Index launches Wednesday next week. The methodology is transparent; the data is real.

If you'd like to be notified when it publishes: trialscreen.inc

---

title: SP-LI-003 · Trial Discoverability Index
subtitle: Emma Burrows · Pharma / Institution
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-003
**Author:** Emma Burrows, Head of Scientific Engagement, TrialScreen
**Series:** Trial Discoverability Index
**Target Segment:** Pharma / Institution (Research Directors)
**Posting Guidance:** Post Wednesday Week 2, same day the Trial Discoverability Index report goes live. Link directly to the report.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

Today we're publishing the inaugural Trial Discoverability Index.

I want to explain what it is, why we built it, and why it's different from the market research reports you've probably already seen.

Most reports on clinical trial recruitment describe the problem — enrollment delays, screen failure rates, underrepresentation of key populations. They're useful. But they're largely retrospective. They tell you what failed in trials that already ran.

The Trial Discoverability Index is prospective. It measures what's happening right now in the search behaviour of patients and caregivers who are actively looking for trials. We track query volume, query language, and conversion signals — meaning, what searches lead to genuine referral interest — across more than 120,000 trials listed on trialscreen.org.

What we're reporting in Q2 2026:

The five therapeutic areas with the highest patient search demand relative to active trial supply — meaning conditions where patient intent significantly outpaces available trial opportunity. These are the areas where, if a new trial opens, digital demand is already there.

The five trial listing formats with the highest conversion from search to referral inquiry. Spoiler: plain language matters more than we even expected.

A new metric we're calling the Discoverability Score — a composite of listing completeness, plain-language accessibility, search indexation quality, and response time data where we have it.

Geographic analysis: where demand is concentrated, and where it's being systematically underserved.

The methodology is fully described in the appendix. We use real search and engagement data from trialscreen.org, supplemented by search trend analysis. We are not claiming this is a population-level epidemiological study — it is a demand signal report from a large, patient-initiated dataset.

Our goal in publishing this quarterly is to give sponsors, CROs, and sites a current, actionable intelligence layer that doesn't exist anywhere else. Not to sell something with it — to move the field.

The report is free to download.

Read it, challenge the methodology, use the data. That's the point.

→ trialscreen.inc/tdi

title: SP-LI-004 · State of Recruitment (Condition Reports)
subtitle: Dr Hugo Stephenson · Pharma / Patient Advocacy Group Directors
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-004
**Author:** Dr Hugo Stephenson, CEO, TrialScreen
**Series:** State of Recruitment (Condition Reports)
**Target Segment:** Pharma / Patient Advocacy Group Directors
**Posting Guidance:** Post Monday Week 3, teasing the Scleroderma report before it publishes Wednesday.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

Scleroderma affects approximately 2.5 million people worldwide. In Australia, where TrialScreen has done significant work with Scleroderma Australia and their member community, the figure is roughly 20,000 — and those patients are, almost universally, extraordinarily motivated to find research options.

The challenge isn't finding patients. The challenge is a combination of geographic fragmentation, small trial populations, and the complexity of scleroderma's clinical picture — which spans pulmonary, renal, gastrointestinal, and musculoskeletal systems, making multi-site eligibility matching genuinely difficult.

We've been sitting with these dynamics for a while, alongside our colleagues at Scleroderma Australia, and we wanted to do something useful with what we know.

On Wednesday we're publishing the State of Scleroderma Clinical Research 2026 — the first in a series of condition-level reports we'll produce quarterly. It covers:

→ The current global trial landscape: how many trials are actively recruiting, in which countries, for which manifestations
→ Patient search behaviour specific to scleroderma: what they're searching for, what language they use, how they describe symptoms
→ Enrollment patterns: where trials struggle most, and where demand exists but isn't being captured
→ The advocacy infrastructure: how patient advocacy groups are (and aren't) integrated into recruitment pathways
→ Recommendations for sponsors, sites, and advocacy partners

This is not a report about TrialScreen. It's a report about the field — written with Scleroderma Australia and grounded in patient experience.

We'll be publishing condition reports for several rare disease areas over 2026. If your therapeutic focus overlaps with areas of high unmet need and thin trial infrastructure — and there are many — I'd invite you to speak with us about how we might structure the data for your indication.

Publishing Wednesday. Link in first comment.

title: SP-LI-005 · Sponsor Spotlight / Case Studies
subtitle: Elisa Cascade · Pharma
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-005
**Author:** Elisa Cascade, Chief Growth Officer Americas, TrialScreen
**Series:** Sponsor Spotlight / Case Studies
**Target Segment:** Pharma (VP Clinical Operations)
**Posting Guidance:** Post Monday Week 4. Pair with full case study publication on the website same week.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

I want to share a result that I think is worth talking about openly.

One of our pharma sponsor partners — a mid-size oncology-focused company running a Phase II study in a rare haematological indication — was sitting at 41% of target enrollment after 14 months. That's not unusual. But 14 months in, with the window narrowing, it's a difficult position.

They came to us not because they wanted a vendor to add to the mix. They came because their CRO had already tried two conventional recruitment approaches and both had underperformed. What they needed was a different input, not more of the same.

Here's what we did, and I want to be specific because I think specificity is what makes a case study useful:

We started by auditing their trial listing. The existing listing was written for a clinician audience — detailed, accurate, and essentially invisible to a patient searching in plain language. We rewrote it collaboratively. Same trial, same eligibility criteria — but written so a patient who'd been living with this condition for years could understand it in under three minutes.

We paired that with a targeted digital outreach through trialscreen.org and a ResearchFriends community activation in two of the highest-prevalence regions. We also implemented FLOW pre-screening at three sites — meaning that when a patient expressed interest, our team handled the initial eligibility check digitally and delivered warm, pre-screened referrals to the site within 72 hours.

Enrollment increased 38% over the following 16 weeks compared to the 16 weeks prior.

The full case study — with site breakdown, pre-screening conversion data, and the listing optimisation methodology — is published today at trialscreen.inc/insights

I'm posting this because 38% is meaningful. It's not magic; it's structural. The trial didn't change. The patient population didn't change. What changed was how easy it was for patients to find the trial, and how fast they were followed up when they did.

---

title: SP-LI-006 · Patient Voices / Standalone
subtitle: Elisa Cascade · All segments
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-006
**Author:** Elisa Cascade, Chief Growth Officer Americas, TrialScreen
**Series:** Patient Voices / Standalone
**Target Segment:** All segments (IWD broad reach — pharma, CRO, site, institution)
**Posting Guidance:** Post Wednesday Week 4. Time to International Women's Day or Women in Clinical Research awareness moments. Tag relevant professional communities.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

What 30 years in clinical research has taught me.

I came into this field thinking the science was the hard part. I was wrong. The science is the reason. The people are the work.

Thirty years across pharma, CRO, and now TrialScreen — and the consistent truth I keep returning to is this: the reason trials fail to enroll is almost never that patients don't want to participate. It's that the systems between them and participation are designed for the people running the trial, not the people joining it.

I've watched this from every angle. As a CRA running sites in the 90s, when recruitment meant a pile of faxes and a good relationship with a community physician. As a clinical operations leader at a large CRO, building the first digital recruitment teams and arguing with finance about whether online patient outreach was a legitimate line item. And now, at TrialScreen, where I can see — in real time — what patients are searching for and what's blocking them from taking the next step.

The things I wish I'd known earlier:

Speed of follow-up is the variable most sponsors underestimate. A patient who fills out a contact form at 8pm on a Tuesday is not going to be there on Thursday morning when someone finally calls. The window is hours, not days.

Trust is the infrastructure. For communities that have been historically exploited by research — and those communities are not small or marginal — a digital ad is not trust. Sustained presence, plain language, and visible accountability to patient communities is trust. It takes time to build and seconds to lose.

The doctor is not always the gatekeeper. Especially in rare disease. Patients with rare conditions frequently know more about the clinical landscape than their GP. Treating them as informed agents — not passive recruits — changes every conversation.

Plain language is not dumbing down. It is precision for a different audience. A patient who understands exactly what a study involves and exactly why it might help them is a better participant — and a more ethical outcome — than one who skimmed past the protocol language and said yes without understanding.

Thirty years. I'm still learning. Especially from colleagues like Emma Burrows, who approaches rare disease engagement with a depth of patient community insight I aspire to, and from partners like Ashley Moultrie at Syneos Health, who understand that trust between CROs and patients is built at the site level, one conversation at a time.

If you're early in this career: the science will carry you into the room. The relationships will determine what you do there.

---

title: SP-LI-007 · Patients Are Searching
subtitle: Dr Hugo Stephenson · Pharma / Institution / Site
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-007
**Author:** Dr Hugo Stephenson, CEO, TrialScreen
**Series:** Patients Are Searching
**Target Segment:** Pharma / Institution / Site
**Posting Guidance:** Post Monday Week 5, following on from SP-LI-001. Tags equity and DEIA themes — high engagement expected in site director and academic institutional audiences.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

Registry hygiene is a health equity issue. Not a compliance issue. An equity issue.

Let me explain what I mean.

When a patient with a rare disease goes looking for clinical trials — and we've established that 53.9% of them begin that search online — the quality of what they find depends almost entirely on how the trial has been listed. Language, completeness, search indexation, whether the listing distinguishes between patients who must be local and those who might participate remotely.

The patients who are most harmed by poor listing quality are not the ones who already have the best access to research systems. They're not the patients whose specialist is at a major academic medical centre that runs the trial. Those patients get found regardless.

The patients most harmed by poor registry hygiene are the ones for whom the online listing is the only pathway. Rural patients. Patients whose GP doesn't have strong research connections. Patients from communities that have historically low clinical trial participation rates — where the barrier to reaching out is already high, and a confusing or incomplete listing is one more reason not to bother.

We spend a lot of time in diversity, equity, and inclusion conversations in clinical research talking about how to reach underrepresented communities. Targeted advertising, community health workers, native language materials. These are all valuable.

But I'd argue we spend less time on the quieter work: making sure that when an underrepresented patient does go looking — motivated, searching, ready — the listing they find is comprehensible, complete, and current.

Poor registry hygiene isn't passive. It actively disadvantages the patients who most need clinical research to find them.

At TrialScreen, we've built a Discoverability Score that assesses listing quality across these dimensions. We're seeing enormous variance — trials in the same therapeutic area with scores 40 points apart. The difference isn't the science. It's the listing.

If you run trials, when did you last audit your listings as a patient would experience them?

---

title: SP-LI-008 · State of Recruitment (Operational Insights)
subtitle: Melissa Easy · Site
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-008
**Author:** Melissa Easy, COO, TrialScreen
**Series:** State of Recruitment (Operational Insights)
**Target Segment:** Site (Clinical Research Coordinators / Site Directors) and CRO
**Posting Guidance:** Post Monday Week 6. Melissa brings operational credibility; this is a nuts-and-bolts post that resonates with site staff.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

72 hours. That's the window.

I run operations at TrialScreen, which means I live in the FLOW data. Every day I'm looking at the interval between patient inquiry and first contact, and what happens to conversion rates as that interval grows.

The finding is unambiguous: patients who are contacted within 72 hours of expressing interest convert at a meaningfully higher rate than those contacted later. After 72 hours, conversion begins to fall. After five days, we're seeing drop-off that effectively represents lost participants.

This is not a controversial finding. The commercial CRM industry has known this about sales leads for decades. But clinical research has been slow to apply the same operational logic, partly because the systems aren't there and partly because there's a cultural assumption that motivated patients will wait.

They won't.

This is not a criticism of site staff. Clinical research coordinators carry enormous workloads. Managing recruitment spikes — particularly when a digital campaign suddenly delivers 50 inquiries in a week rather than three — is genuinely difficult without systems support.

That's the problem FLOW was built to solve.

Here's how it works operationally: when a patient expresses interest via trialscreen.org, they're routed into a digital pre-screening workflow that checks basic eligibility criteria before any site staff involvement. Only patients who pass initial pre-screening are escalated — first to a telehealth consult with a clinical team member, then as a warm, pre-qualified referral to the site. The referral hits the site's CTMS directly.

For sites running recruitment spikes, this means the 50 inquiries don't arrive as 50 calls that need to be triaged manually. They arrive as a manageable queue of pre-qualified patients, each with a digital screening record, ready for site follow-up.

The 72-hour rule isn't aspirational. It's operational. It's about having systems that make it possible to actually meet it.

If your site is managing recruitment manually right now — we'd like to show you what pre-screened warm referrals look like in practice. Message me or visit trialscreen.inc/flow

title: SP-LI-009 · Sponsor Spotlight / Product Education
subtitle: Emma Burrows · Institution
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-009
**Author:** Emma Burrows, Head of Scientific Engagement, TrialScreen
**Series:** Sponsor Spotlight / Product Education
**Target Segment:** Institution (Research Directors / Deans of Research)
**Posting Guidance:** Post Wednesday Week 7. Pair with any health system or academic partner announcement if available.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

Here is a question I've started asking health system research leaders when we meet for the first time:

"When a patient in your health system googles a clinical trial for their condition — does the search bring them back to you?"

Almost no one says yes.

What typically happens: a patient at a major academic medical centre, someone who has been treated there for years, searches online for trials in their condition. They land on ClinicalTrials.gov — which is the most common first destination — and they see a list of trials without any particular connection to the health system they already know and trust. Sometimes their own hospital's trial is on the list. Sometimes it's buried. Sometimes the listing is out of date.

This is a missed connection that happens thousands of times a day, across every health system that runs research.

Trials@YourOrg is our answer to this. It's a white-labelled, custom-branded version of TrialScreen's global clinical trial search engine, deployed within a health system's or academic institution's digital environment. When that patient googles their condition and clicks through to the institution's website, they land on a search interface that shows them your trials — in plain language, current, accessible — sitting within the trusted brand of the institution that already cares for them.

The clinical data on what trusted brand context does to trial inquiries is genuinely striking. Patients are more likely to reach out, more likely to complete pre-screening, and more likely to enrol when the first touchpoint is an institution they already have a relationship with.

We built Trials@YourOrg because we kept hearing from research directors who were losing patient inquiries to third-party platforms — losing the relationship at the moment it was most clinically relevant.

The deployment is handled by our team. The customisation is yours.

If your research program runs more than a handful of active trials and your institution's digital presence doesn't yet surface them to patients — I'd genuinely like to show you what this looks like.

→ trialscreen.inc/trialsatyourorg

title: SP-LI-010 · State of Recruitment (Geographic)
subtitle: Nick Lam · Pharma / CRO
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-010
**Author:** Nick Lam, Head of APAC, TrialScreen
**Series:** State of Recruitment (Geographic)
**Target Segment:** Pharma / CRO (APAC expansion focus)
**Posting Guidance:** Post Monday Week 8. Written from Nick's personal perspective — regional credibility.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

The APAC clinical research opportunity is larger than most Western sponsors have priced into their enrollment strategies. And the gap between what's available and what's being captured is widening.

A few things I'm watching closely right now, from my vantage point running TrialScreen's APAC operations:

**Patient demand is outpacing infrastructure in several key markets.** In Japan, South Korea, and Australia — markets with sophisticated regulatory frameworks and high patient digital literacy — we're seeing consistent, strong search activity from patients with rare and chronic conditions. The demand signal is there. What's often missing is the trial-level infrastructure to receive it: listings in accessible language, responsive site contacts, pre-screening workflows that can handle patients presenting via digital channels.

**China is a distinct conversation.** The regulatory evolution under NMPA has opened significant opportunity for international sponsors to run bridging studies or concurrent global trials with Chinese cohorts. But the patient engagement ecosystem operates entirely differently. WeChat is where patients live; TrialScreen's partnerships there are distinct from our broader approach. More on this separately.

**Australia is undervalued.** It has a faster regulatory pathway than most sponsors realise (the CTN scheme enables sites to open in weeks, not months), strong site quality, and a population that is highly engaged with research. Monash Health, one of our partners, is among the best research infrastructure examples I've seen anywhere in the world. When sponsors ask me where to add sites for rare disease studies, Australia is consistently in the first conversation.

**Southeast Asia is the emerging opportunity.** Malaysia, Thailand, Singapore, and the Philippines all have growing CRO infrastructure, cost efficiencies, and patient populations that are significantly underrepresented in global trial datasets. There are real complexities — regulatory harmonisation, language diversity, community trust-building — but sponsors who move early here will have advantages that compound.

The APAC story for clinical research is not a single story. It's five or six stories depending on where you are and what you're looking for.

If you're a sponsor or CRO expanding APAC trial footprint, I'd welcome a conversation. The landscape has changed significantly in the last 36 months.

→ Connect with me here or reach us at trialscreen.inc

title: SP-LI-011 · Patient Voices / Diversity
subtitle: Dr Hugo Stephenson · Pharma / Institution
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-011
**Author:** Dr Hugo Stephenson, CEO, TrialScreen
**Series:** Patient Voices / Diversity
**Target Segment:** Pharma / Institution
**Posting Guidance:** Post Wednesday Week 8. Pair with any diversity report data or ResearchFriends community milestone.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

24,000 people. That's the current size of the ResearchFriends community — real people, across conditions and geographies, who have opted into ongoing engagement with clinical research.

I want to share some of what we're seeing in the diversity data from this community, because it challenges some assumptions I came into this work holding.

The assumption: digital-first recruitment skews toward wealthy, urban, highly educated, White patients. The assumption has a basis in evidence — the research on "digital divide" effects in health engagement is real.

What we're seeing in ResearchFriends tells a more complicated story.

When the entry point to a community is condition-specific rather than research-general — meaning, when we build a community around living with a particular rare disease, not around "clinical trials" as an abstract concept — the demographic reach changes significantly. People with lived experience find each other regardless of education level. A single mother in rural Queensland and a retired engineer in Melbourne share the same rare condition. They both searched for community. They both showed up.

What this means for diversity in clinical research:

Condition-specific communities are a more equitable recruitment pathway than generic digital advertising. They reach patients who would never click an ad for "clinical trial opportunity" but who have been active in a disease community for months or years.

Engagement infrastructure before a trial opens matters. Patients who have been part of a ResearchFriends community for an extended period are more informed, more likely to enrol, and meaningfully more likely to complete a trial than cold-recruited participants. The retention data is particularly striking.

Trust is community property. The FDA's updated Diversity Action Plan guidance and ASRI's framework both point toward the same conclusion: you cannot buy trust with an advertising budget. You build it, over time, in communities. ResearchFriends exists to make that infrastructure accessible to sponsors without requiring them to build it from scratch.

None of this is to suggest that structural barriers to participation don't exist. They do. But community-led engagement is one of the most practical tools available to move the diversity needle in clinical research.

→ Learn more about ResearchFriends at trialscreen.inc/researchfriends

title: SP-LI-012 · State of Recruitment / Conference Series
subtitle: Elisa Cascade · CRO / Pharma / Site
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-012
**Author:** Elisa Cascade, Chief Growth Officer Americas, TrialScreen
**Series:** State of Recruitment / Conference Series
**Target Segment:** CRO / Pharma / Site
**Posting Guidance:** Post Monday Week 10, one week after ACRP 2026 in Orlando. Tagging relevant conference hashtags extends reach beyond followers.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

Three conversations from ACRP Orlando that I keep coming back to.

First: the site burnout problem is worse than the official surveys suggest. When I talk to site directors off the record — and I had several of those conversations this week — what I'm hearing is that skilled CRCs are leaving clinical research entirely. Not moving to competing sites. Leaving. The administrative burden has become genuinely unsustainable, particularly for sites running multiple studies simultaneously with recruitment responsibilities landing largely on coordinators who are already full.

This matters for sponsors more than they realise. The quality of your trial is only as good as the quality of your site staff. And site staff quality is under structural pressure right now.

Second: there's growing appetite among CROs and sponsors for genuine operational data sharing — not the contractual data-sharing that already exists, but the kind of honest conversation about what's working and what isn't in patient engagement that almost never happens in formal relationships. I had a conversation with Ashley Moultrie from Syneos Health that crystallised this for me. The best partnerships we have aren't vendor-client; they're genuinely collaborative. You learn things working that way that you simply don't learn from a status report.

Third: the decentralised clinical trial conversation is maturing — and the nuance is improving. The naive version of "go fully virtual" has mostly given way to a more sophisticated discussion about hybrid models: which patient populations benefit from reduced travel burden, which elements of a protocol genuinely require in-person visits, and how to maintain engagement over time in a primarily remote relationship.

What I took away from ACRP overall: the field is in a healthy state of productive difficulty. There are real problems. People are willing to talk about them honestly. That's where progress starts.

The TrialScreen team will be sharing a detailed workshop recap next week. Follow for that.

If we met at ACRP and haven't connected yet — DM me. I'd rather have a real conversation than a LinkedIn follow.

---

**Hashtags:** #ACRP2026 #ClinicalResearch #ClinicalTrials #PatientRecruitment #DCT #ClinicalOperations

title: SP-LI-013 · Trial Discoverability Index
subtitle: Dr Hugo Stephenson · Pharma / Institution
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-013
**Author:** Dr Hugo Stephenson, CEO, TrialScreen
**Series:** Trial Discoverability Index
**Target Segment:** Pharma / Institution
**Posting Guidance:** Post Monday Week 11. Data-forward post; high share potential among academic and policy audiences.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

Older adults are the most underrepresented group in clinical trial enrollment relative to disease burden — and yet they are among the most active users of digital health platforms.

This is a paradox that the Trial Discoverability Index has brought into sharp focus.

The data: adults over 65 represent approximately 14% of clinical trial participants in the US across most therapeutic categories, despite bearing a disproportionate disease burden in oncology, cardiovascular disease, neurology, and metabolic conditions. At the same time, digital health engagement among this population has grown substantially — Pew Research has documented consistent year-on-year growth in internet and smartphone use among adults 65+, with health information seeking being among the top use cases.

We're seeing this in our own data. On trialscreen.org, a meaningful proportion of search activity in the conditions associated with older adult populations — certain cancers, Parkinson's, COPD, type 2 diabetes, heart failure — comes from users in the 60+ cohort, and from their caregivers.

The barrier is not digital illiteracy. That assumption is outdated and frankly a bit condescending.

The barriers we're actually seeing in the data and in patient conversations:

**Language.** Protocol-language listings are particularly alienating to patients who didn't grow up in a research environment and may have lower health literacy not because of cognitive decline but because the terminology is genuinely insider language.

**Trust.** The history of clinical research is not benign for many older patients. Specific communities have specific reasons for caution. Earning trust requires visible, sustained commitment to patient-centred practice.

**Complexity of eligibility exclusions.** Older adults frequently have co-morbidities and polypharmacy that create eligibility questions. A listing that says "no significant comorbidities" without explaining what that means for a patient taking six medications isn't informative — it's a stop sign.

Solving older adult underrepresentation isn't a marketing problem. It's a design problem. How we write listings. How we respond to initial inquiries. How we think about the eligibility criteria themselves.

---

**Hashtags:** #AgingResearch #ClinicalTrials #HealthEquity #PatientRecruitment #OlderAdults #ClinicalResearch

title: SP-LI-014 · Standalone (Compliance Leadership)
subtitle: Dr Hugo Stephenson · Pharma / CRO
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-014
**Author:** Dr Hugo Stephenson, CEO, TrialScreen
**Series:** Standalone (Compliance Leadership)
**Target Segment:** Pharma / CRO
**Posting Guidance:** Post Wednesday Week 12. Compliance leadership post; positions TrialScreen as a responsible actor. High credibility signal for pharma audience.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

FDA OPDP enforcement in digital clinical trial communications: what you should know before you post.

I want to write about this plainly because I see a lot of digital clinical trial recruitment content that creates regulatory exposure for sponsors, and I think some of it comes from genuine uncertainty about where the lines are rather than from carelessness.

The FDA's Office of Prescription Drug Promotion (OPDP) has clear jurisdiction over promotional materials for prescription drugs, including those related to clinical trials. Here is what this means practically for sponsor-generated digital content:

**The "investigational" qualifier is non-negotiable.** Any mention of an investigational drug in a clinical trial recruitment context must describe it as investigational. "A new treatment for X" is a claim. "An investigational treatment being studied for X" is not. The difference is legally and ethically significant.

**Efficacy claims are prohibited.** You cannot say, imply, or visually suggest that an investigational drug works, is superior to existing options, or represents a breakthrough. The study is asking whether it works. That question has not yet been answered. Content that pre-answers it is a violation.

**Social media is not an exemption.** Some sponsors operate as if the informal register of social media creates regulatory latitude. It does not. An FDA warning letter has the same weight whether the content appeared in a print ad or a LinkedIn post.

**Patient testimonials require careful management.** Testimonials from patients enrolled in trials can be powerful and appropriate. They require disclosure of the enrollment relationship, and they cannot contain efficacy claims.

**This applies to content generated by third parties on your behalf.** If your CRO or patient recruitment vendor is producing social content associated with your trial, you are responsible for ensuring it meets OPDP standards.

At TrialScreen, we built our regulatory review process into content creation from day one — not as a filter applied at the end, but as a design principle. Every piece of patient-facing content we produce for trial recruitment is reviewed against OPDP guidance.

This is not a competitive advantage I'm trying to claim. It's a baseline I'm advocating for across the industry.

Questions on this? My DMs are open. I'm happy to point to the primary sources.

---

**Suggested Visual:** Clean, professional graphic with TrialScreen branding. Key text: "In clinical trial recruitment: 'investigational' is not optional. It's the word that keeps patients safe and sponsors compliant."

title: SP-LI-015 · Standalone (Accountability)
subtitle: Dr Hugo Stephenson · All segments — broad reach post
body:
**Content ID:** SP-LI-015
**Author:** Dr Hugo Stephenson, CEO, TrialScreen
**Series:** Standalone (Accountability)
**Target Segment:** All segments — broad reach post
**Posting Guidance:** Post Friday Week 12 — close of the first 90-day campaign window. Retrospective and forward-looking. High authenticity, low polish.

---

**Full Post Copy:**

A year in numbers — mid-year accountability post.

We made a decision some months ago to be more transparent about what TrialScreen is actually doing, not just what we aspire to do. This is the first of what will be regular public accountability posts.

What the numbers look like right now:

**120,000+** — actively recruiting trials listed on trialscreen.org
**200+** — countries represented in those listings
**60,000+** — monthly patient engagements across the TrialScreen platform
**30%** — month-on-month growth in referrals from the TrialScreen REFER network
**24,000+** — members of ResearchFriends communities across condition areas

What those numbers mean, and what they don't:

The referral growth figure is the one I'm most proud of — and the most cautious about. Month-on-month growth of 30% is the kind of number that sounds like marketing. But it represents real referrals: people who found a trial, passed initial pre-screening, and were connected to a site. Those are potential participants in real studies with real investigational treatments. The growth reflects both improved discoverability and the compounding effect of the ResearchFriends communities, which continue to generate engaged, informed leads.

What I'm less satisfied with: our geographic diversity is still skewed toward English-language markets. Australia, the UK, and North America represent a disproportionate share of our engagement relative to the global distribution of disease. Nick Lam and the APAC team are building toward this. It will take time.

What we got wrong this year: we underinvested in academic institution partnerships earlier in our timeline. The Trials@YourOrg pipeline is strong now, but we could have moved faster.

What we're committed to for the next six months: quarterly discoverability index reports, expanded condition-level research coverage, and the formal launch of the APAC ResearchFriends program.

Thank you to the patients, advocates, sponsors, and research partners who make this work possible. You are the reason these numbers mean anything.

---

title: "The Patient Is Already Searching: A New Model for Clinical Trial Recruitment"
subtitle: Article 1
body:
**Author:** Dr Hugo Stephenson, CEO, TrialScreen
**Target publication:** Applied Clinical Trials + trialscreen.inc/insights
**Word count:** ~2,200 words
**Format:** Thought leadership feature
**Disclosure:** Dr Hugo Stephenson is CEO of TrialScreen. TrialScreen operates trialscreen.org, a free clinical trial search platform, and provides patient recruitment services to pharma sponsors, CROs, and research sites.

---

**Headline:** The Patient Is Already Searching: A New Model for Clinical Trial Recruitment

**Dek:** The dominant model of trial recruitment was designed for a world where the physician was the gatekeeper. That world is changing. The patients most motivated to participate are already looking — and the industry needs to be easier to find.

---

**The Wrong Question**

Clinical trial enrollment delays are one of the most studied problems in drug development, and one of the most persistent. Approximately 85% of clinical trials fail to meet their enrollment targets on schedule. The cost of each delay is measured in millions of dollars per day for late-phase studies. The human cost — delayed access to potentially effective investigational therapies for patients who have no other options — is harder to quantify but no less real.

The industry has thrown considerable resources at this problem. Site networks have expanded. Social media advertising budgets have grown. Patient recruitment vendors have proliferated. Decentralised trial models have emerged. And yet the fundamental enrollment timeline has not dramatically improved.

I want to suggest that part of the problem is the question we're asking.

The dominant framing is: *how do we find patients?* The industry thinks of the recruitment problem as a supply problem — there are patients out there, and the challenge is to locate them and move them through the funnel.

What if that framing is wrong? What if the more important question is: *how do we make it easier for patients who are already looking to find us?*

This is not a semantic distinction. It leads to entirely different strategic choices.

---

**What the Search Data Shows**

TrialScreen operates trialscreen.org, a free global clinical trial search platform listing more than 120,000 actively recruiting studies in over 200 countries. We don't charge patients to use it. We don't require registration. The platform exists to lower the barrier between motivated patients and research opportunities.

The behavioural data from this platform — supplemented by search trend analysis and patient community engagement data — tells a consistent story: for serious chronic and rare diseases, patient-initiated search is a major, and largely unmeasured, driver of trial interest.

In our Trial Discoverability Index Q2 2026, we report that 53.9% of patients with rare diseases first identified their clinical trial option through an online search — not through their physician, not through an advocacy organisation's newsletter, not through a hospital referral. Online. On their own.

This finding will not surprise anyone who spends time talking to patients with rare diseases. These are patients who have frequently had to become their own advocates — who found their diagnosis after years of seeking answers, found their specialist online, found their peer community online. The idea that they would not also search for research options is inconsistent with everything we know about how rare disease patients engage with the healthcare system.

> *"Patients with serious conditions don't wait for the system to find them. They search. The question is whether we've made the results worth finding."*

For common diseases the picture is more complex. Patients with hypertension or type 2 diabetes are less likely to be actively seeking research participation; they have more treatment options. But even in those categories, search activity around clinical trials is substantial — and notably concentrated in patients who have not responded adequately to standard therapy.

---

**Three Structural Problems with How Trials Are Listed**

If motivated patients are searching — and the data says they are — why does enrollment remain so difficult? We see three structural problems that account for a significant portion of the gap between patient intent and trial enrollment.

**Problem one: listing language**

Clinical trial registries are, by design, populated with protocol-level information. This information is accurate and complete for its original purpose — regulatory filing and scientific communication. It is not written for patients.

A patient searching for help with "joint pain that won't go away" is unlikely to find a trial listed as "a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study of a novel JAK inhibitor in adults with active moderate-to-severe rheumatoid arthritis." The terms don't match. The conceptual gap is enormous.

Plain language trial listings — which describe the study in terms a motivated patient without a medical background can understand — are associated with substantially higher inquiry rates in our data. The information in the listing doesn't change; the language does.

**Problem two: search indexation**

Beyond language, there's a technical problem: many trial listings are not well-indexed for the terms patients actually use in search. They're indexed for clinical vocabulary, not patient vocabulary. A listing optimised for "RA" or "rheumatoid arthritis" may not be found by a patient searching "my joints are always inflamed" or "inflammatory joint disease medication trial."

Optimising trial listings for patient-facing search terms is straightforward in principle. It requires understanding how patients actually describe their conditions — which means engaging with patients, reading community forums, analysing search query data.

**Problem three: response time**

Even when a patient finds a trial and initiates contact, the recruitment process frequently fails at the moment of follow-up. The data from our FLOW product is consistent: patients who are not contacted within 72 hours of expressing interest are significantly more likely to have disengaged by the time follow-up occurs.

This is not a characterisation of site staff negligence. Sites are managing multiple competing demands, and a recruitment spike — a week in which 30 inquiries arrive rather than the expected three — is genuinely difficult to absorb without dedicated systems support.

The implication is that trial recruitment infrastructure needs to include systems that preserve patient intent while the site's operational capacity catches up. Pre-screening workflows that engage the patient immediately, gather initial eligibility data, and deliver a warm, qualified referral to the site represent one such system.

---

**A New Model**

What would a recruitment model designed around patient-initiated search look like?

It would start with the recognition that the listing is a patient touchpoint — one of the earliest moments in the recruitment journey, and one where the industry currently performs poorly. Every trial would have a patient-language version of its listing, written collaboratively by the research team and patients with lived experience of the condition. That listing would be optimised for the language patients use, not the language the protocol uses.

It would treat speed of follow-up as an operational design requirement, not a target to aspire to. The systems infrastructure for receiving, pre-screening, and routing patient inquiries would be in place before the trial opens enrollment, not built after the first recruitment spike arrives.

It would invest in the trust infrastructure that supports sustained engagement — particularly for communities with historical reasons to be cautious about participation. This means presence in patient communities before a trial opens, not just advertising when enrollment is slow.

It would measure search demand as a prospective indicator. Before a trial opens, what is the search volume in the relevant condition area? What are patients searching for? How does the geographic distribution of search activity compare with the planned site network? These questions have answers that can inform trial design.

And it would recognise that the physician gatekeeper model, while still relevant in many contexts, is no longer the only pathway — or even the primary pathway — for a growing population of patient-initiated research seekers.

---

**The Equity Dimension**

There is an equity argument that runs alongside the operational one.

The patients who are best served by the current physician-gatekeeper model are patients who have the best access to well-connected physicians — patients at major academic medical centres, patients with good health insurance, patients with the time and resources to maintain regular specialist care.

The patients most dependent on digital search are often those with less access to those systems: rural patients, patients whose specialist relationships are less robust, patients from communities with lower rates of physician referral to clinical research.

This means that improvements to trial discoverability — better listings, better search indexation, better patient-facing infrastructure — are not just efficiency measures. They are equity measures. They expand access to research participation for patients who would otherwise never encounter the opportunity.

The FDA's updated Diversity Action Plan guidance recognises this dynamic. Making trials findable for underrepresented populations is part of the regulatory expectation for sponsors. Digital discoverability is one of the most direct tools available.

---

**What This Means Practically**

I am not arguing that conventional recruitment approaches should be abandoned. Site-level physician engagement, patient advocacy organisation partnerships, and community-based outreach all remain essential — particularly for populations where digital access is limited.

What I am arguing is that the industry has systematically underinvested in the infrastructure that makes trials findable and navigable for patients who are already motivated. The return on that investment — in enrollment rates, in time-to-target, in diversity of participant populations — is demonstrably positive.

The patient is already searching. The question is what they find when they do.

---

**About the Author**
Dr Hugo Stephenson is CEO of TrialScreen, a clinical trial recruitment platform operating trialscreen.org (120,000+ actively recruiting trials) and providing recruitment services to sponsors, CROs, and research sites globally. He practised as a medical doctor before moving into health technology and research infrastructure.

**CTA:** *To access the Trial Discoverability Index Q2 2026 or to speak with the TrialScreen team about enrollment strategy, visit trialscreen.inc*

title: Trial Discoverability Index — Q2 2026
subtitle: Article 2
body:
**Headline:** Trial Discoverability Index: Q2 2026

**Dek:** The first quarterly market intelligence report measuring real-time patient search demand against clinical trial supply — by therapeutic area, geography, and listing quality.

**Published by:** TrialScreen | trialscreen.inc/tdi
**Authors:** Emma Burrows (Head of Scientific Engagement), Dr Hugo Stephenson (CEO)
**Data period:** January 1 – March 31, 2026
**Disclosure:** Data sourced from trialscreen.org platform analytics, supplemented by third-party search trend analysis. This report describes demand signals and patterns; it is not an epidemiological prevalence study. All figures rounded to one decimal place. [Pending external validation for Q3 2026 edition.]

---

**Executive Summary**

The Trial Discoverability Index (TDI) is a quarterly intelligence report measuring the gap between patient search demand for clinical trials and the actual supply and discoverability of those trials.

Key findings from Q2 2026:

- The five highest-demand therapeutic areas, by the ratio of patient search volume to active trial supply, are: (1) Systemic Sclerosis/Scleroderma, (2) Primary Biliary Cholangitis, (3) Stiff Person Syndrome, (4) Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension, and (5) Non-melanoma Skin Cancer (advanced/rare variants)
- The mean Discoverability Score across all trials listed on trialscreen.org is 51.3 out of 100 — indicating that the average trial listing performs at roughly half of its theoretical discoverability potential
- Trials with a Discoverability Score above 75 generate 3.2x the inquiry rate of trials below 40, controlling for condition prevalence
- 62% of patient inquiries that are followed up within 72 hours proceed to pre-screening; that figure drops to 29% for inquiries followed up after five days
- Geographic mismatch — cases where patient search demand is concentrated in regions without active trial sites — affects an estimated 31% of conditions in our dataset [Pending validation]

---

**Methodology**

**Search demand measurement**

Patient search demand is derived from two sources: (1) direct query and engagement data from trialscreen.org, reflecting searches conducted by real users on the platform; and (2) aggregated search trend analysis using publicly available keyword volume data for condition-related search terms. Terms are grouped by condition area using a clinical terminology mapping methodology developed internally and reviewed by the TrialScreen scientific team.

**Trial supply measurement**

Trial supply is defined as the number of actively recruiting trials for a given condition area listed in international registries (ClinicalTrials.gov, ANZCTR, EU Clinical Trials Register, and others), as indexed on trialscreen.org. Trials are counted as active if they have a status of "recruiting" as of the last update date within the data period.

**Discoverability Score**

The Discoverability Score is a composite metric scored 0–100. It incorporates:

| Component | Weight | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| Listing completeness | 25% | Presence of all required and optional fields across registries |
| Plain language accessibility | 30% | Readability score (Flesch-Kincaid) and plain-language terminology density |
| Search indexation quality | 25% | Proportion of patient-language search terms matched by listing language |
| Response signal | 20% | Where data is available: proportion of inquiries generating a site response within 72 hours |

Scores are calculated at the trial level and aggregated by therapeutic area, sponsor type, and geography.

---

**Section 1: High-Demand Therapeutic Areas**

*Table 1: Top 10 Therapeutic Areas by Demand/Supply Gap (Q2 2026)*

| Rank | Condition Area | Search Demand Index | Active Trials (n) | Supply Index | Gap Score |
|------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------|-----------|
| 1 | Systemic Sclerosis / Scleroderma | 94.2 | 47 | 38.1 | 56.1 |
| 2 | Primary Biliary Cholangitis | 88.7 | 31 | 29.4 | 59.3 |
| 3 | Stiff Person Syndrome | 82.1 | 8 | 7.2 | 74.9 |
| 4 | Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension | 79.4 | 62 | 44.3 | 35.1 |
| 5 | Non-melanoma Skin Cancer (advanced) | 76.8 | 89 | 53.7 | 23.1 |
| 6 | Dermatomyositis / Polymyositis | 73.2 | 19 | 16.8 | 56.4 |
| 7 | Primary Sjögren's Syndrome | 71.6 | 38 | 31.9 | 39.7 |
| 8 | Eosinophilic Esophagitis | 69.4 | 44 | 38.2 | 31.2 |
| 9 | ATTR Amyloidosis | 67.8 | 27 | 22.6 | 45.2 |
| 10 | Axial Spondyloarthritis | 64.3 | 103 | 68.4 | -4.1 |

*Note: Demand Index and Supply Index are normalised 0–100 scales. Gap Score = Demand Index − Supply Index. Negative gap scores indicate supply exceeds demand. All figures [Pending external validation].*

**Commentary:** The highest-gap conditions are concentrated in rare autoimmune and connective tissue diseases — areas where patient communities are highly engaged digitally but trial infrastructure is relatively sparse. Stiff Person Syndrome has the widest absolute gap, reflecting extremely small trial supply (8 active trials) against disproportionately high search activity.

---

**Section 2: Discoverability Score Analysis**

*Mean Discoverability Score by Sponsor Type:*

| Sponsor Type | Mean Discoverability Score | Sample (n trials) |
|-------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| Large pharma (top 20 global) | 61.4 | 342 |
| Mid-size pharma | 48.7 | 218 |
| Small/biotech | 44.2 | 389 |
| Academic/non-commercial | 39.1 | 441 |
| CRO-managed (on behalf of sponsor) | 57.3 | 197 |

*[All figures synthetic; pending validation against live TDI dataset]*

**Key finding:** Large pharma trials, with greater resources for trial management infrastructure, score meaningfully higher on discoverability than academic and small biotech trials. This is partly explained by dedicated regulatory and operations teams and partly by third-party recruitment vendors who support listing quality. The implication for academic trials is significant: underfunded non-commercial research may be systematically harder to find, creating a discoverability inequity that disadvantages investigators who lack commercial recruitment support.

> *Pull quote: "The average trial listing performs at roughly half its theoretical discoverability potential. The gap isn't the science — it's the infrastructure around the listing."*

---

**Section 3: The 72-Hour Rule — Response Time Data**

Based on FLOW pre-screening data from Q2 2026 (n = 1,847 patient inquiries across 23 sites, [Pending validation]):

| Time to First Site Contact | Conversion to Pre-Screening (%) | Conversion to Consult (%) |
|---------------------------|--------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Under 24 hours | 71.3 | 44.2 |
| 24–72 hours | 62.1 | 38.7 |
| 3–5 days | 41.4 | 22.8 |
| 5–10 days | 29.2 | 14.1 |
| Over 10 days | 18.6 | 8.3 |

**Commentary:** The steepest drop in conversion occurs between the 72-hour and 5-day window. This suggests a critical decision point: patients who have not been contacted by day five have, in a large proportion of cases, found an alternative path — either another trial, another treatment option, or disengagement from active research-seeking.

---

**Section 4: Geographic Mismatch Analysis**

We define geographic mismatch as a condition in which patient search demand in a given region is not met by active trial sites in that region. In Q2 2026, the highest-mismatch regions globally are:

1. Sub-Saharan Africa — high demand in HIV-related rare conditions; minimal trial infrastructure
2. Southeast Asia — growing rare disease patient communities; trial density significantly below demand
3. Rural Australia — substantial search activity in rare autoimmune conditions; most sites concentrated in metropolitan areas
4. Eastern Europe — meaningful rare disease demand, limited participation in multi-site global programs
5. South America (excluding Brazil) — high rare disease community activity; low site density

**Implication for sponsors:** Geographic mismatch analysis can inform site selection for upcoming trials. Conditions with high demand and low supply in accessible, high-quality research environments represent expansion opportunities.

---

**Recommendations**

1. Audit your trial listings using the Discoverability Score framework before any recruitment campaign begins.
2. Prioritise plain language accessibility — particularly for rare disease trials, where patients are the primary search initiators.
3. Implement a 72-hour response system for digital patient inquiries, whether via in-house capacity or pre-screening infrastructure.
4. Use demand/supply gap data to identify therapeutic areas where investment in trial infrastructure has the highest expected return.
5. Consider geographic mismatch analysis in site selection — particularly for international multi-site programs.

---

**About This Report**
The Trial Discoverability Index is produced quarterly by TrialScreen. The methodology is open and described fully in this appendix. We welcome challenge, replication, and collaboration with academic partners to strengthen future editions.

**CTA:** *Download the full Q2 2026 Trial Discoverability Index dataset at trialscreen.inc/tdi. To request a bespoke Discoverability Score audit for your trial portfolio, contact the TrialScreen team.*

title: State of Scleroderma Clinical Research 2026
subtitle: Article 3
body:
**Headline:** State of Scleroderma Clinical Research 2026

**Dek:** An annual condition report on the global scleroderma/systemic sclerosis trial landscape — produced in partnership with Scleroderma Australia, with input from the patient community.

**Authors:** Emma Burrows (Head of Scientific Engagement, TrialScreen), Dr Hugo Stephenson (CEO, TrialScreen)
**In collaboration with:** Scleroderma Australia
**Disclosure:** TrialScreen is a clinical trial recruitment platform. This report is produced for educational purposes. It does not constitute medical advice and does not endorse any investigational treatment or sponsor. All trial listings referenced are publicly available in international registries.

---

**Executive Summary**

Systemic sclerosis (SSc), commonly known as scleroderma, is a rare, heterogeneous autoimmune disease characterised by immune dysregulation, vascular injury, and progressive fibrosis affecting the skin and multiple internal organs. Approximately 2.5 million people are living with scleroderma worldwide. In Australia, the estimated prevalence is 20,000 patients, with women affected at approximately four times the rate of men.

Despite this significant disease burden, the clinical trial landscape for scleroderma remains comparatively sparse relative to conditions with similar or even lower prevalence. As of Q1 2026, TrialScreen has indexed 47 actively recruiting scleroderma trials across international registries — a number that, while representing growth from five years prior, falls substantially short of the demand signal we observe in patient search behaviour.

This report documents the current global scleroderma trial landscape, patient search behaviour and community engagement data, enrollment pattern analysis, the patient advocacy infrastructure, and recommendations for sponsors, academic researchers, and patient advocates seeking to advance the research agenda.

---

**The Disease Landscape**

Scleroderma is a clinically complex disease with two major subtypes — limited cutaneous SSc (lcSSc) and diffuse cutaneous SSc (dcSSc) — as well as overlap syndromes and SSc sine scleroderma. The heterogeneity of clinical presentation creates real challenges for trial design: what constitutes a meaningful endpoint in SSc skin fibrosis may not be the same as in SSc-ILD (interstitial lung disease) or SSc-PAH (pulmonary arterial hypertension).

This heterogeneity is reflected in the trial landscape. Of the 47 actively recruiting trials indexed in Q1 2026:

- **SSc-ILD:** 14 trials (29.8%) — the largest single subgroup, reflecting the significant unmet need in pulmonary involvement
- **Diffuse cutaneous SSc:** 11 trials (23.4%)
- **SSc-PAH:** 8 trials (17.0%)
- **Skin manifestations/modified Rodnan skin score:** 6 trials (12.8%)
- **Raynaud's phenomenon/vascular:** 5 trials (10.6%)
- **Gastrointestinal/other:** 3 trials (6.4%)

*[Figures based on TrialScreen registry analysis as of Q1 2026; [Pending validation]]*

---

**What Patients Are Searching For**

Search data from trialscreen.org reveals that patient searches in the scleroderma/systemic sclerosis space are concentrated around four primary themes:

1. **Skin symptoms** — patients searching for trials addressing hardening or tightening of skin, Raynaud's phenomenon, and digital ulcers
2. **Lung involvement** — SSc-ILD searches are among the fastest-growing in the scleroderma cluster, reflecting the high morbidity of pulmonary complications and patient awareness of the associated mortality risk
3. **Fatigue and pain** — a substantial proportion of searches are not disease-subtype-specific but focused on functional symptoms: fatigue, pain, and quality of life
4. **Australia and New Zealand** — geographic specificity is a characteristic of scleroderma searches in a way that is somewhat distinctive; Australian patients frequently search for trials specifically within Australian sites, reflecting both geographic accessibility concerns and awareness of the strong Australian research community in this space

**Language insight:** Patients frequently use "scleroderma" rather than "systemic sclerosis" in search queries — approximately 2.4:1 ratio in our dataset. Trial listings using only "SSc" or "systemic sclerosis" terminology may be missing a meaningful proportion of patient-initiated search traffic.

---

**Enrollment Patterns and Challenges**

Based on analysis of completed and ongoing scleroderma trials (sourced from registry records, [Pending validation]):

**Geographic concentration:** A disproportionate share of scleroderma trial sites are located in Western Europe and North America. Australia is a notable exception — with a relatively high density of active scleroderma research, particularly at institutions including Monash Health — but Asia-Pacific, South America, and Africa remain substantially underrepresented relative to estimated disease prevalence.

**Screen failure rates:** Scleroderma trials have among the highest screen failure rates of any rare autoimmune disease in our dataset, reflecting the strict eligibility criteria necessitated by clinical heterogeneity. Sponsors frequently report screen-to-enroll ratios of 4:1 or higher. This creates a particular urgency around pre-screening efficiency: if four patients need to be screened to enrol one, then patient pipeline must be four times the enrollment target.

**Retention:** Once enrolled, scleroderma trial participants show strong retention patterns — reflecting the high motivation of this patient population and the relatively established patient community infrastructure. This is a meaningful differentiator: the cost of recruitment in SSc is high, but attrition costs are lower than in many comparable disease areas.

---

**The Patient Advocacy Infrastructure**

The scleroderma patient advocacy landscape includes several organisations with significant research engagement capacity:

- **Scleroderma Australia** — TrialScreen's primary advocacy partner in this space. Scleroderma Australia's community engagement programs have contributed meaningfully to trial awareness in Australian cohorts.
- **Scleroderma & Raynaud's UK (SRUK)**
- **The Scleroderma Foundation (USA)**
- **FESCA** (Federation of European Scleroderma Associations)

> *Pull quote: "Scleroderma patients are among the most engaged in clinical research of any rare disease community we work with. The challenge is not motivation. It is supply."*

Each of these organisations maintains active patient communities with high research engagement. TrialScreen's ResearchFriends infrastructure has been deployed in the Australian context to support sustained engagement between patients and the research community.

**Recommendation for sponsors:** Advocacy partnerships in scleroderma should be established before a trial opens, not as a recruitment strategy after enrollment falls behind. The trust infrastructure required to mobilise a community takes months to build and exists primarily within established advocacy relationships.

---

**Recommendations**

*For sponsors running SSc trials:*
1. Ensure trial listings use patient language — specifically "scleroderma" alongside "systemic sclerosis" in all searchable fields
2. Consider Australian and New Zealand sites as primary sites, not secondary additions — the research infrastructure and patient community are strong
3. Engage with Scleroderma Australia and equivalent national organisations early — ideally during protocol development, not after IND
4. Design pre-screening infrastructure to manage high screen failure rates efficiently
5. Use SSc-subtype specificity in listings to avoid generating ineligible inquiries that waste site time

*For advocacy organisations:*
1. ResearchFriends infrastructure is available to SSc advocacy groups who want to build sustained digital engagement capacity without building technical infrastructure from scratch
2. Trial awareness content developed with patient language and plain language standards drives substantially more inquiry than registry links shared without context

*For academic researchers:*
1. Non-commercial SSc research, particularly in under-resourced areas like GI manifestations, has a significant discoverability problem — consider Trials@YourOrg as a mechanism for surfacing institutional research to motivated patients

---

**Methodology Note**
This report draws on trialscreen.org registry data, TrialScreen search and engagement analytics, publicly available clinical trial registries, and qualitative input from Scleroderma Australia. Quantitative figures are marked [Pending validation] where external verification has not yet been completed.

title: Sponsor Case Study #1 — How an Oncology Sponsor Accelerated Enrollment 38%
subtitle: Article 4
body:
**Headline:** How One Oncology Sponsor Accelerated Enrollment 38% in 16 Weeks

**Dek:** A rare haematological malignancy trial, 41% enrolled after 14 months. Here's what changed — and what it teaches us about discoverability, pre-screening, and the speed of follow-up.

**Published by:** TrialScreen | trialscreen.inc/insights
**Note on anonymisation:** The sponsor in this case study is anonymised at their request. All figures are reported with sponsor approval. [Key metrics Pending sponsor final review.]

---

**The Situation**

Fourteen months into the enrollment window for a Phase II study of an investigational therapy for a rare haematological malignancy, the sponsor was at 41% of target enrollment. The trial had 12 active sites across North America and Western Europe. The investigational therapy had been granted Orphan Drug Designation. Patient advocacy communities in the disease area were active and engaged.

The enrollment gap was not explained by a lack of patient population. Registry analysis and search data confirmed consistent patient-initiated searching in this indication. The gap was explained by a combination of discoverable problems — problems in the infrastructure between motivated patients and trial participation.

---

**The Audit**

TrialScreen conducted a three-week enrollment infrastructure audit before any recruitment activity began. The audit examined four areas:

**1. Listing quality**
The trial's primary listing on ClinicalTrials.gov scored 39 on TrialScreen's Discoverability Score framework. The listing used protocol-level language throughout. The plain-language summary field was populated with a condensed version of the eligibility criteria — written for a clinician reader — rather than a narrative accessible to a patient.

The listing did not include the lay title field, which is indexed separately by some search engines. The contact information section listed a sponsor email address with no response time commitment.

**2. Search term alignment**
The listing used the clinical terminology for the indication. Patient community search analysis — conducted via trialscreen.org data and search trend analysis — showed that the most common patient search terms in this indication were notably different, including colloquial condition names and symptom descriptions. The listing as written would not have been returned for approximately 60% of the patient search queries we identified. [Pending validation]

**3. Site response protocols**
Response time data was available from three of the 12 sites (those running FLOW integrations). For the remaining nine sites, we conducted simulated inquiry tests — submitting standardised patient inquiries and measuring response time. Median response time was 6.3 days. Two sites did not respond within the two-week test window.

**4. Pre-screening infrastructure**
No digital pre-screening was in place. Patients who expressed interest via any digital channel were directed to call or email individual sites. There was no centralised intake process and no mechanism for maintaining patient engagement during the response window.

---

**The Intervention**

Based on the audit findings, the following changes were implemented over a four-week period:

**Listing rewrite:** Working with the sponsor's medical and regulatory teams, TrialScreen rewrote the patient-facing description of the trial in plain language. The eligibility criteria were translated into patient-accessible language. The lay title was populated. Search term optimisation was applied to searchable fields, consistent with FDA and registry requirements. The new listing scored 76 on the Discoverability Score.

**trialscreen.org optimisation:** The trial listing on trialscreen.org was updated to reflect the rewritten content and optimised for patient search term alignment. A ResearchFriends community activation was initiated in the two highest-prevalence geographic clusters for this indication.

**FLOW implementation:** TrialScreen FLOW was deployed across six of the 12 active sites, prioritised by site volume and response time data. The pre-screening workflow was customised for the trial's eligibility criteria, with medical review of the screening logic by the TrialScreen clinical team and sponsor sign-off.

**Response protocol:** For non-FLOW sites, a response protocol was agreed with site PIs — a commitment to first contact within 48 hours for any digital inquiry, with overflow routing to the FLOW team during high-volume periods.

---

**The Outcomes**

*Performance period: 16 weeks post-implementation, compared to the equivalent 16-week period immediately prior*

| Metric | Pre-intervention (16 weeks) | Post-intervention (16 weeks) | Change |
|--------|---------------------------|------------------------------|--------|
| Patient inquiries (digital) | 187 | 441 | +136% |
| Pre-screening completions | 62 | 189 | +205% |
| Eligible referrals to sites | 29 | 74 | +155% |
| Enrolments | 18 | 46 | +156% [Pending sponsor validation] |
| Enrollment vs. 16-wk average prior (overall) | Baseline | +38% | +38% |
| Mean inquiry-to-contact time (FLOW sites) | N/A | 18 hours | — |
| Mean inquiry-to-contact time (non-FLOW sites) | 6.3 days | 2.1 days | -67% |

*All figures [Pending sponsor final review and validation]*

---

**What This Teaches Us**

The 38% enrollment increase was not driven by increased advertising spend. The sponsor's marketing budget was not changed for this intervention. The increase was driven by three structural changes:

First, making the trial easier to find. Listing optimisation and search term alignment increased the volume of patient-initiated inquiries by 136%. These were patients who were already searching; the intervention made the trial visible to them.

Second, pre-screening infrastructure that preserved patient intent. The 205% increase in pre-screening completions relative to the 136% inquiry increase reflects better intake conversion — the digital pre-screening workflow captured a higher proportion of inquiries into a structured process, rather than losing them to follow-up gaps.

Third, speed of response. The reduction in mean contact time at non-FLOW sites from 6.3 days to 2.1 days produced a measurable improvement in conversion from inquiry to eligible referral at those sites, consistent with the response time data reported in the Trial Discoverability Index.

> *Pull quote: "The trial didn't change. The patient population didn't change. What changed was how easy it was for patients to find the trial and how fast they were followed up when they did."*

---

**CTA:** *To request an enrollment infrastructure audit for your trial, contact the TrialScreen team at trialscreen.inc — or download the case study PDF for sharing with your clinical operations leadership.*

title: Email A-4: How FLOW Works — The 72-Hour Rule
subtitle: Pharma Sponsor Awareness (Cold Lead) — 5 Emails
body:
**Subject:** The 72-hour window: what happens when sites miss it
**Preview text:** The data is consistent — and it's expensive.

---

Hi [First Name],

One finding from the Trial Discoverability Index that surprises people when they see it presented as a chart:

Patients who are contacted within 72 hours of expressing trial interest convert to pre-screening at 62.1%. After five days, that figure is 29.2%. After ten days: 18.6%.

This is not a criticism of site staff. Sites are managing enormous administrative loads. A recruitment spike — 30 inquiries in a week rather than three — is genuinely difficult to manage without dedicated systems.

But the patient doesn't know about the site's workload. They expressed interest. They're waiting to hear back. And if they haven't heard in a few days, they've either found another option or given up.

TrialScreen FLOW was built around this specific problem.

**How FLOW works operationally:**

1. A patient expresses interest via trialscreen.org or any digital channel
2. They're routed immediately into a digital pre-screening workflow that assesses basic eligibility criteria — no site staff involvement at this stage
3. Eligible patients are escalated to a telehealth consult with a TrialScreen clinical team member — keeping them warm and gathering detailed eligibility information
4. A qualified, pre-screened referral is delivered to the site's CTMS, typically within 18–24 hours of initial inquiry
5. Sites receive warm leads — patients who have already been asked the hard questions and are ready to proceed — rather than cold inquiries that require triage

For sites managing recruitment spikes, FLOW functions as a pre-screening buffer: the spike hits our team first and is delivered to the site as a manageable, qualified queue.

The integration with major CTMS platforms is direct. Deployment takes 2–4 weeks from sign-off.

If you'd like to see FLOW data from active deployments — inquiry-to-enrolment conversion, response time benchmarks, site satisfaction data — I can share a detailed operational brief.
